author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Does Pelagius Designedly Refrain from Openly Saying that All Good Action is from God?
title: Does Pelagius Designedly Refrain from Openly Saying that All Good Action is from God?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 17 [XVI.]—Does Pelagius Designedly Refrain from Openly Saying that All Good Action is from God?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: “That we are able to speak,” says he, “is of God; but that we make a good or a bad use of speech is of ourselves.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: He, however, who has made the most excellent use of speech does not teach us so.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: “For,” says He, “it is not ye that speak, but the Spirit of your Father that speaketh in you.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: “So, again,” adds Pelagius, “that I may, by applying a general case in illustration, embrace all,—that we are able to do, say, think, any good thing, comes from Him who has endowed us with this ability, and who also assists it.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Observe how even here he repeats his former meaning —that of these three, capacity, volition, action, it is only the capacity which receives help.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Then, by way of completely stating what he intends to say, he adds: “But that we really do a good thing, or speak a good word, or think a good thought, proceeds from our own selves.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: He forgot what he had before said by way of correcting, as it were, his own words; for after saying, “Man is to be praised therefore for his willing and doing a good work,” he at once goes on to modify his statement thus: “Or rather, this praise belongs both to man, and to God who has given him the capacity of this very will and work.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now what is the reason why he did not remember this admission when giving his examples, so as to say this much at least after quoting them: “That we are able to do, say, think any good thing, comes from Him who has given us this ability, and who also assists it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: That, however, we really do a good thing, or speak a good word, or think a good thought, proceeds both from ourselves and from Him!”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: This, however, he has not said.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But, if I am not mistaken, I think I see why he was afraid to do so.
